name: Should I repair my existing sliding glass door or replace it completely?
headline: Should I repair my existing sliding glass door or replace it completely?

mainEntity.name: Should I repair my existing sliding glass door or replace it completely?
mainEntity.text: Should I repair my existing sliding glass door or replace it completely?

acceptedAnswer.text: If your sliding glass door is hard to open, wobbly, or lets in drafts, you don’t always need a full replacement. But sometimes replacing the whole unit is cheaper and safer than trying to patch a failing door. **Repair might be enough** when: - The door sticks because the **track is dirty** or slightly bent - The **rollers are worn** but the frame and glass are in good shape - Weatherstripping is damaged but the door still closes square These issues can often be fixed by cleaning, adjusting, or replacing rollers and seals. **Replacement makes more sense** when: - The frame is warped, rotten, or heavily corroded - Glass is fogged between panes (failed seal) or cracked - The door won’t square up or lock properly even after adjustment - You are dealing with major drafts or water leaks Older doors also tend to be far less energy‑efficient, so a new door can make the room more comfortable and may lower heating costs. A common pitfall is pouring money into repeated small repairs on a door that really needs replacement, or replacing a door when there’s actually a framing or foundation issue causing the problem. If you’re unsure, get someone to inspect not just the door, but also the sill and surrounding structure before deciding.
